retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia With Variant Genetics -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to genetic abnormalities, certain gene mutations are vital to CLL pathogenesis, and multiple subpopulations of evolving malignant cells have been identified. [27] [28] These alterations affect intracellular or microenvironment-dependent signaling pathways. Genes involving intracellular signaling pathways include those affecting DNA damage (TP53, ATM, POT1), chromatin modifiers (ASXL1, SETD2, HIST1H1B, HIST1HIE, BAZ2A, ZMYM3, SYNE1, CHD2, ARID1, KMT2D), RNA splicing and metabolism (XPO1, MED12, SF3B1, CNOT3, U1, FUBP1, DDX3X, RPS15, ZNF292). Genes involving microenvironment-dependent signaling include the MAPK-ERK pathway (PTPN11, MAP2K1, KRAS, BRAF, NRAS), Notch signaling (FBXW7, NOTCH1), NF-kB signaling (EGR2, TRAF2, TRAFR3, NFKB2, NRKBIE, BIRC3, NKAP), and B cell receptor (BCR) and Toll-like receptor signaling (IRF4, BCOR, TLR2, KLHL6, IRAK1, PAX5, MYD88). [29] [30] [27] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The KDM6A mutation affects normal growth and development through similar mechanisms of KMT2D , as both genes belong to the same multiprotein complex. KDM6A (also known as UTX ) is the second most pathologic gene, found in less than 5% of patients with Kabuki syndrome. [1] This mutation is inherited in an X-linked manner; the gene is located on Xp11.23 and encodes a histone (H3)-lysine 27-specific demethylase that interacts with KMT2D in regulating gene expression in the activating signal cointegrator-2-containing complex (ASCOM). [1] [3] [5] Mutations of KDM6A include nonsense, frameshift, splice site, missense, large deletions, small insertions, and small deletions; a rare mosaic case of KDM6A has previously been identified. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A study even reported that NPM1 mutations were present in up to 35% of patients with AML. [14] Intermediate-risk AML is diagnosed in the presence of any FLT3-ITD mutation or t(9;11)(p21.3;q23.3, or MLL:KMT2A rearrangement). Lastly, high-risk AML categorization can be diagnosed in the presence of several cytogenetic or molecular aberrancies, which notably include monosomy 5/del 5q or 7/deletion 7q, other monosomal or complex karyotype (≥3 unrelated abnormalities), or mutations in ASXL1, EZH2 , SRSF2, or TP53 .

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: AML with t(8;21)(q22;q22.1); RUNX1-RUNX1T1 AML with inv(16)(p13.1q22) or t(16;16)(p13.1;q22); CBFB-MYH11 APL with t(15;17)(q22;q12); PML-RARA AML with t(9;11)(p21.3;q23.3); MLLT3-KMT2A AML with t(6;9)(p23;q24); DEK-NUP214 AML with inv(3)(q21.3q26.2) or t(3;3)(q21.3;q26.2); GATA2, MECOM AML (megakaryoblastic) with t(1;22)(p13.3;q13.3); RBM15-MKL1 AML with mutated NPM1 AML NOS includes the following: AML with minimal differentiation AML without maturation AML with maturation Acute myelomonocytic leukemia Acute monoblastic/monocytic leukemia Pure erythroid leukemia Acute megakaryoblastic leukemia Acute basophilic leukemia Acute panmyelosis with myelofibrosis

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- High Risk of Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: Certain genetic mutations and markers indicate a significantly elevated risk of relapse. For instance, AML patients with complex karyotypes, monosomy 7, or specific translocations such as t(6;9) or t(3;3) are classified as high-risk. [68] Similarly, ALL patients with hypodiploidy or the presence of MLL ( KMT2A ) gene rearrangements face a high risk of relapse. In these cases, aggressive treatment strategies are essential, including the consideration of early HSCT. [60] [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: In 2016, World Health Organization (WHO) revised the classification and categorized it into AML with recurrent genetic abnormalities, AML with myelodysplasia-related changes, therapy-related myeloid neoplasms, NOS, myeloid sarcoma, myeloid proliferations related to Down syndrome. AML with recurrent genetic abnormalities includes- AML with t(8;21)(q22;q22.1); RUNX1-RUNX1T1 AML with inv(16)(p13.1q22) or t(16;16)(p13.1;q22); CBFB-MYH11 APL with t(15;17)(q22;q12); PML-RARA AML with t(9;11)(p21.3;q23.3); MLLT3-KMT2A AML with t(6;9)(p23;q24); DEK-NUP214 AML with inv(3)(q21.3q26.2) or t(3;3)(q21.3;q26.2); GATA2, MECOM AML (megakaryoblastic) with t(1;22)(p13.3;q13.3); RBM15-MKL1 AML with mutated NPM1 AML with biallelic mutations CEBPA AML not otherwise specified (NOS) includes:

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphoblastic Lymphoma -- Pathophysiology -- B-Lymphoblastic Leukemia/Lymphoma with Recurrent Genetic Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: B-ALL/LBL with t(v;11q23.3); KMT2A rearrangement: This entity shows a translocation between the KMT2A ( MLL ) gene at band 11q23 and up to 100 different fusion partners. This entity usually presents with a pro-B immunophenotype. ALL with KMT2A rearrangements is by far more common in infants younger than 1 year and tends to present more often with leukocytosis and central nervous system (CNS) involvement compared to other entities. The most common fusion partner gene is AF4 on chromosome 4q21. Overall, B-ALL with KMT2A rearrangements cases has an unfavorable outcome compared to other ALL entities.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of acute promyelocytic leukemia. Outline the presentation of a patient with acute promyelocytic leukemia. Describe the treatment options available for acute promyelocytic leukemia. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the diagnosis and treatment of acute promyelocytic leukemia and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosomal translocations: Recent studies have identified new fusion genes in ALL, such as DUX4 rearrangements and KMT2A mutations in infant ALL, highlighting the heterogeneity of this disease. Research is focused on understanding how these fusion genes disrupt gene expression and signaling pathways, driving leukemic transformation. [28] [29] Gene mutations: Gene mutations are a central factor in the development and progression of ALL, with both loss-of-function and gain-of-function mutations contributing to leukemogenesis. Loss-of-function mutations: Recent studies have identified novel tumor suppressor genes involved in ALL, such as TP53 , RB1 , and CREBBP . [20] [30] These mutations disrupt cell cycle regulation, DNA damage repair, and epigenetic control, thereby contributing to uncontrolled cell growth and survival.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Bone Marrow -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Acute lymphoid leukemia subtypes are also defined based on molecular genetics in the WHO 2022 classification. Cytogenetics and FISH are efficient diagnostic tools for identifying the defining chromosomal alterations (eg, BCR::ABL1, KMT2A::AFF1, ETVG::RUNX1, TCF3::PBX1, and iAMP21 ), and prognostic factors like aneuploidy. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Interfering Substances
retrieved_docs.content: Certain medications, such as heparin and specific antibiotics, may interfere with flow cytometry analysis by causing nonspecific binding or altering antigen expression. Additionally, high levels of paraproteins, as seen in conditions such as multiple myeloma, can disrupt immunophenotypic analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Jacobsen syndrome is a contiguous gene deletion disorder caused by a partial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 11. The size of the deletion varies, typically ranging from 7 to 16 Mb. [3] [4] Deletions larger than 20 Mb are expected to be lethal, but some cases of large deletions have been found in mosaic form, presenting as a severe phenotype. [5] Terminal deletions of chromosome 11 occur in 2 ways—a de novo pure terminal deletion (85% of cases) or an unbalanced familial translocation (15% of cases). Chromosomal breakage typically occurs within or beyond subband 11q23.3, often extending to the telomere. This partial chromosomal deletion results in the loss of all genes located distal to the breakpoint, which typically includes genes CBL2 , FRA11B , TECTA , BSX , NRGN , FEZ1 , CHK1 , ETS1 , FLI1 , KCNJ1 , BARX2 , NFRKB , APLP2 , ADAMTS8 , ADAMTS15 , OPCML , JAM3 , and B3GAT1 . Subband 11q23.3 includes FRA11B , a gene with a fragile site prone to double-stranded breakage, which serves as a breakpoint in some patients with Jacobsen syndrome. Smaller interstitial deletions within the Jacobsen syndrome region can also cause the condition, even without a full terminal deletion. [6] [7] [8] Regardless of the mode of pathogenicity, chromosomal deletions in Jacobsen syndrome can have incomplete penetrance and an unpredictable genotype-to-phenotype correlation.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer Tissue-Based Biomarkers -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Deletions in APC, CHD1, ETS2, NKX3.1, and SETD2. Deletions/mutations in ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, ERF, KMT2A, KMT2C, KMT2D, KDM1A, KDM3A, KDM6A, NCOR1, NCOR2, PTEN, RB1, SMAD4, SMARCA1, SMARCB1, and TP53. Amplification/mutation/splice variant mutations in AR. Fusion/deletion in ERG and  ETVs. Mutations in EZH2, FOXA1, IDH1, and SPOP. Amplification in MYC, MYCN, and SETDB1 .

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Etiology of Acute Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: Acute leukemia, which includes both ALL and AML, results from the malignant transformation of hematopoietic progenitor cells. This transformation typically arises from a multifactorial process involving a complex interaction of genetic and environmental factors. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis in AML depends on an individual patient's cytogenetic and molecular characterization. A favorable-risk AML, for instance, can be diagnosed in the presence of translocation of specific chromosomal material, including t(8;21), t(15;17), and inversion of chromosome 16, or t(16;16). Higher-risk cytogenetic aberrancies or mutations, such as t(6;9)(p23.3;q34.1) or mutations in ASXL1 and U2AF1 , generate a higher risk and indicate a less favorable prognosis. Adverse outcomes have been noted with older age, WBC count (>100,000 at diagnosis), secondary or therapy-related AML, and the presence of leukemic cells in the central nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective communication and collaboration among clinicians, pathologists, and laboratory scientists are essential for optimal patient care. Regular multidisciplinary discussions support the interpretation of complex laboratory findings, promote appropriate test utilization, and ensure the implementation of personalized treatment plans. Integrating molecular testing into the diagnostic process enhances understanding of the genetic landscape of acute leukemia, identifies prognostic markers, refines risk stratification, and informs the selection of targeted therapies, ultimately enabling more effective treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant are the abnormal cells that define cancer of kidney and renal pelvis, and this specific malignancy is mapped to the Arabic term سرطان الخلايا الكلوية. Recognizing that malignant tumor cells constitute the pathological cellular basis of this renal and pelvic cancer and linking the English disease name to its Arabic equivalent supports accurate diagnosis, interdisciplinary communication, and culturally appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1503
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous is classified as a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, a pharmacologic agent that specifically targets the BCR‑ABL fusion proteins (bcr‑abl fúzové proteiny). By inhibiting these oncogenic fusion proteins, dasatinib anhydrous interferes with the constitutive tyrosine kinase activity that drives malignant cell proliferation in diseases such as chronic myeloid leukemia, making it a key component of targeted therapy strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2018
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, and imatinib are classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As members of this targeted‑therapy class, each of these agents may be used to treat lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي). The relationship indicates that the BCR‑ABL inhibitor class encompasses dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib, all of which share the therapeutic indication of managing lymphoid leukemia, highlighting their role in targeted treatment strategies for this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: by multiple Arabic terms (الورم الأورمي الكلوي, سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) and are treated surgically by partial or complete nephrectomy, inversely related to the Arabic procedure term استئصال الكلية; the co‑occurrence of nephrectomy with these renal cancer terms highlights the therapeutic link. Overall, the network provides clinicians with a structured framework for recognizing secondary lesions, interpreting malignant infiltrates, categorizing metastatic spread by organ and histology, and differentiating benign from malignant cellular findings to guide patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 247
retrieved_docs.content: specified sites, malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث), melanomas of skin (a subtype of malignant skin cancer that also encompasses malignant lip cancer), prostate adenocarcinoma, squamous‑cell carcinoma of the head and neck, lung carcinoma of non‑smokers, and generic metastatic cancer. Melanomas of skin have been reported with a finding site in the retina, highlighting possible ocular involvement. Collectively, these hierarchical and inverse‑is‑a relationships create an integrated network that connects hereditary syndromes, primary organ‑specific cancers, and diverse metastatic presentations to the central concept of malignant tumors and secondary malignancies, supporting precise diagnosis, classification, and management of oncologic patients.

Together, these relationships demonstrate how the classification integrates diverse malignant neoplasms, connects specific tumor subtypes to broader malignant categories, identifies anatomical sites of disease extension, and relates morphological descriptors and clinical findings across organ systems, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.
